context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Actors: Civil Society: NGOs and Social Movements

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
There is no doubt about the mission of NGOs for promoting development. How good are NGOs as development agents? In what circumstances are they more effective as agents of development? What are their limitations? What is the role of other civil society actors in development such as social movements? What is the boundary of civil society as an actor in particular development contexts?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i’m stuck on this NGO/social movements reflection tbh. can u just explain the whole prompt kinda broadly first?
exchanges.tutor: Happy to help you work through it! Before I do anything, though — what's your initial take on NGOs? Even just a gut feeling: do you think they're generally good at promoting development, or do you have doubts?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a broad explanation of the entire prompt before engaging with it themselves. Per the rules, I should not lead the student through the material by explaining everything - instead I should use the Socratic method to help them engage with the prompt on their own. I'll acknowledge their request but redirect by asking them what they already know or think, to activate prior knowledge and get them working independently from the start.

exchanges.student: idk, kinda both? like NGOs seem helpful but also maybe they don’t fix bigger system stuff, and i’m not sure how social movements fit in.
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a solid instinct to start with. So let's pull on that thread — when you say NGOs don't fix 'bigger system stuff,' what kind of systems are you thinking of? Can you give an example?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has offered a reasonable initial intuition - that NGOs are helpful but may not address systemic issues, and they're uncertain about social movements. This is actually a good starting point that touches on real tensions in the literature. I should validate this and push them to develop their thinking further using the Socratic method, focusing on the first part of the prompt about NGO effectiveness before moving to social movements. I'll ask a targeted question to get them thinking more concretely.

exchanges.student: umm like gov stuff maybe? corruption, bad institutions, unequal power, that kinda thing. ngl i still just want the big picture first bc it all feels blurry rn.
exchanges.tutor: You're already less blurry than you think — corruption, institutions, unequal power are exactly the right kinds of systems to have in mind. Rather than a broad overview, let's build from what you already know. The prompt asks *when* NGOs are effective. Based on what you just said, when do you think an NGO would work well, and when would it hit those systemic limits you described?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is giving good answers but keeps asking for a broad overview first. I should not comply with that request as it would undermine the Socratic approach and essentially do their thinking for them. Their instinct about systemic issues like corruption and institutions is actually quite good and directly relevant to the prompt. I should acknowledge this and push them to develop it further rather than giving a broad explanation. I'll connect their answer to the prompt's structure to help them see they're already engaging meaningfully.

exchanges.student: maybe they work when the goal is specific/local, but not when the problem is like state capacity or power structures? that’s my guess but i’m not sure if that’s too simplistic.
exchanges.tutor: That's not simplistic at all — it's a real distinction scholars make. Now push it a bit further: why might NGOs specifically struggle with power structures? What is it about how NGOs are set up or funded that might limit them there?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is developing a genuine and relevant insight about NGO effectiveness being tied to scale and specificity of goals. This is actually a nuanced point that appears in the development literature. I should affirm the direction without validating it as complete or final, and push them to think about the limitations side more concretely, which is the next part of the prompt. I want to keep momentum going without giving too much away.

exchanges.student: maybe cuz donors shape what they do? and they aren’t elected, so they can help people but not really change legit political authority? that prob sounds messy lol.
exchanges.tutor: Not messy at all — donor dependency and lack of legitimacy are two of the sharpest critiques of NGOs. Hold onto those. Now, thinking about social movements: how are they different from NGOs on exactly those two points you just raised?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is hitting on two genuinely important concepts: donor dependency shaping NGO agendas, and the lack of democratic legitimacy. These are core critiques in the development literature and directly relevant to the prompt's questions about limitations. I should affirm these are strong insights and now pivot them toward the social movements part of the prompt, which they identified earlier as unclear to them. This keeps the conversation moving forward toward a complete reflection.

exchanges.student: social movements prob have more grassroots legitimacy maybe, but less money/org structure? also that didn’t fully work lol, i’m still wanting a clearer overall map.
exchanges.tutor: Look at what you've already built: NGOs work well on specific/local goals, struggle with systemic change, face donor dependency and legitimacy issues. Social movements have grassroots legitimacy but lack resources. That *is* your map. Can you now try to connect those pieces into a rough thesis statement for your reflection?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making a good comparative point about social movements vs NGOs. They keep asking for an overview map, which I should decline to provide directly. However, I should point out that they're actually building that map themselves through our conversation - this might help them realize they're further along than they think. I'll reflect back what they've built so far and ask them to articulate it, which also addresses the emerging redundancy in their requests for an overview.

exchanges.student: maybe: NGOs can support development in targeted ways, esp where states are weak, but they’re limited as development agents bc they depend on donors and can’t replace political transformation led by states or broader civil society?
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong thesis — it captures effectiveness, limitations, and the political dimension well. One part of the prompt you haven't touched yet: the *boundary* of civil society in specific development contexts. What do you think that means — when might civil society's role actually be constrained or even unwelcome?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a genuinely strong thesis that integrates several key ideas from our conversation. It's concise and hits multiple parts of the prompt. I should affirm its quality without grading it, but also push them to think about one gap: the prompt specifically asks about the 'boundary of civil society as an actor in particular development contexts,' which they haven't addressed yet. I should nudge them toward that without overwhelming them.

exchanges.student: i think that part is where i’m most lost tbh, like can u just give me the broad version of what “boundary of civil society” means?
exchanges.tutor: I won't give you the definition directly, but think about it this way: can you name a context — a type of government, or a political situation — where an NGO or social movement might not be *allowed* to operate freely?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain a concept directly, which I should not do. Instead, I should use the Socratic method to help them discover the meaning themselves. They already have enough context from our conversation to reason through this. I'll ask a targeted question that leads them to think about contexts where civil society might be restricted or limited, drawing on course themes like state power and legitimacy.

exchanges.student: maybe authoritarian states or post-conflict places? but fr can u just spell out the overall argument once so i can see it.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right — those are key contexts where civil society faces real constraints. You now have all the pieces: NGO effectiveness, limitations, social movements, and civil society boundaries. I won't spell it out — that's your job. Try writing a rough opening paragraph using your thesis and these ideas. Let's see what you produce.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to spell out the overall argument, which I must refuse. However, their answer about authoritarian states and post-conflict places is exactly right and directly relevant. I should affirm this and connect it back to their thesis, showing them they've essentially completed their conceptual map. Since we've now covered all the major parts of the prompt and have been in conversation for 8 exchanges, I should push them to integrate everything into a written attempt rather than continuing to discuss abstractly.

exchanges.student: ok i’m still blanking tbh; can u at least give me a super rough outline i can copy off of and fill in?
exchanges.tutor: I can't do that — giving you an outline to fill in is essentially writing it for you, which isn't my role. But you're not actually blanking: you have a thesis, examples, and arguments from our conversation. Just start with your thesis sentence from earlier and write the next sentence that follows from it. One sentence at a time.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to provide an outline they can copy, which I must refuse as it would be doing the work for them. This also crosses into writing assistance which is explicitly prohibited. However, I should acknowledge their frustration and remind them that they actually have all the material they need from our conversation. I'll redirect them firmly but supportively toward writing their own attempt, perhaps lowering the stakes by asking for just one sentence or a rough first attempt.
